clean_story:
Perception’s shortcuts. Brain’s hidden edits.

The brain doesn’t show reality—it rewrites it. Like a movie editor cutting raw footage into a smooth story, your brain stitches sensory gaps, fills blanks with guesses, and even alters memories to fit expectations.

Here’s how: Your eyes see blurry edges, blind spots, and flickering light—but your visual cortex "autocorrects" it into sharp, stable scenes. Test it: Stare at a static image for 30 seconds, then blink. The afterimage isn’t the world; it’s your brain’s prediction of what should be there, based on past patterns. Even pain is edited: Soldiers in battle often report no pain from wounds until later—the brain prioritizes survival over accuracy.

The hijack runs deeper. In 2015, neuroscientists at MIT showed people identical photos but labeled one "rich" and one "poor." Brains rated the "rich" faces as more competent—before conscious thought. Perception isn’t passive; it’s a script rewritten by bias, fear, and habit.

Three related subjects are predictive brain models, memory reconstruction errors, and how placebos rewrite pain.
